entry.content:
Andreas is a cofounder of frontier AI lab - Black forest labs. Black forest labs has a lot of open models, and also does some consulting, where they provide specialized visual AI models to companies.

Andreas origin story

Andreas did an undergrad in mechanical engineering, and wanted to focus on AI for his PhD. He joined a small lab and studies visual model representation learning in visual modes.

Back in this day, it was all about who could write the paper. Andreas’ lab competed with companies like Google and OpenAI. His lab was quite small so they had to be strategic on what they compete on with big wigs who had much more resources.

His lab focused on finding representations that are equivalent to the pixel space, but much more efficient than tokens (make compute more efficient). This lead to latent generative models, which gave rise to stable diffusion, outdoing competitors.

After this success, his labs next goal was purely focusing on building an image model that is 10 times better than current options. In fact his team spent 3 months just on Flux 1.

Now, he mentions that we need models that learn and interact like us, from natural representations (talking, visual, etc.). He advocates for multimodal models that will outdo everything and it (in theory) can provide a deeper natural understanding of physics as the model can use ANYTHING in the real world as an training example.

The Best Image Model: Creation of Flux

Flux’s training has pre, mid, and post-training.

Pre training

  • has text and images

Mid training

  • adds higher resolution and more capabilities

Post training

  • includes offline distillation to be more efficient and match customer needs before deployment

From systems perspective: Feedback loop is clsoed when the tech is used to solve real problems, providing data on what users care about. Ex. Flux improved character consistency within 60 days, when they released Flux 1 Context, a special editing model.

Next wave of models

Andreas says the purpose of shifting toward multimodal models is to move beyond content creation towards physical AI, robotics, and world simulation.

Historically, it has been difficult to get models to understand semantics. Ex. when using a representation learning pretrained model, they were restricted with trying to match the transformer backbone to another model. This technique was limited to a single modality (unimodal), such as just images Because it relied on these specific pre-trained “anchors,” the system was restricted to that specific domain and could not easily scale to understand how different types of data (like audio and video) relate to each other

Self Flow was discussed as a way of solving multimodal reasoning.

Self Flow

While previous alignment techniques were restricted to a single modality, Self Flow allows a model to align its internal representations across multiple modalities.

This is done during pre-training to build a general representation of the world before adding specific tasks like “action prediction” (e.g., predicting a keystroke or robot movement) during mid-training.

Andreas reccomends this data labeling strategy:

  • Pretraining (quantity): use lots of noisy data. Labels handled automatically
  • Post-training (quality): shift to high quality data and human signals.

Iterative denoising and model differences

Both diffusion and language models are iterative but:

Diffusion: iterative in an artificial time dimension that moves from noise to image.

  • This is data inefficient - each example generate infinite potential losses across noise to data trajectory (you can distil from any time point)
    • But we can distil diffusion models better than language
    • BFL use latent adversarial distillation to reduce steps to generate image from 50 to 4 steps (we generate image extremely fast)

Language: iterative in direction of data, generating one token at a time. Tokens can be parallized so its data efficient - Con: Inference takes lots of time

(Future) How do we build spatial intelligence in 3D world

Andreas believes that all a model needs to understand space is the ability to observe natural representations (video) and interact with objects. 3D representations are notoriously difficult - Existing 3D representations are narrow, inflexible, and static (point clouds) - while a model might develop an implicit 3D structure within its neural weights to understand the world, the interface for learning and interaction should remain natural (video/audio) rather than being forced through explicit 3D priors

Overall thoughts

It is a common sentiment, but its clear Andreas agrees with the idea that a multimodal model should have a single transformer backbone rather than hack a bunch of good LLMs, VLMs, together.

On the 3D debate, Andreas mentions the importance of focusing on working with EXISTING training data over trying to make your own data sets. (this point interests me: i feel like there many companies focused on making datasets, which are doing quite well.) I guess his point his to make a foundation model it needs to be able generalize. Then at that point specialized datasets become valuable. Cool.

the trend with andreas: he says with frontier AI you optomize inference via architectural distillation, sicne image models used to be really data inefficeitn and slow at inference times.

I got into looking at these lectures by looking at the lineup of speakers they had. It was completely stacked with big names from industry (Sam Altman, Ben Horowitz, Jensen, etc.). But the comment section says its just ‘AI Coachella’, meaning nothing of substance is being said, and its mainly for maintaining the interviewee’s image.

I decided to take a look at it myself, is there really of anything of substance said?

The first lecture is:

Lets takea field-trip into the 2026 Frontier AI factory

In 2026 the way we make infrastructure for AI is changing.

The AI scaling loop is get compute -> get data -> model results, put the money back into compute. Bottlenecks include how much compute you have, the public’s perception, and how much capital you have Most companies (like in YC) follow these three steps:

  1. incubation (focus on what part of the system you want to attack)
  2. SOTA release
  3. expand model to more capabilities / diversify offerings

Its fun to take apart new companies and see how many are actually following this roadmap.

THE ultimate scaling law:

  • More compute turns into better models, which turns into better revenue From a systems perspective, you can convert between infra and compute: $1 of infrastructure turns into $1 of revenue. GUARANTEED. WOW what a claim.

Think about it. Literally every big company is exponentially investing in infrastructure. For a reason right? Example: The H100 GPU is a 2 year old chip, but the cost to use them is peaking NOW.

  • Computing chips is not just a commodity that depreciates over time

examples of products that followed this law:

Steel: Hoarded until industrialization caused prices to crash. Fiber Optics: Over-expansion led to internet transit prices dropping 90% in 5 years. DRAM: Panic/world events trigger infrastructure sell-offs before the market stabilizes. Uranium: Value peaked, crashed after major incidents, and was ultimately stabilized by government intervention. Stabilization Timeline: Historically takes 3 years for digital commodities and 6 years for physical infrastructure.

Compute is Not Yet a Commodity

Unlike traditional commodities, compute is currently non-fungible and incredibly difficult to forecast. This means chips are not interchangeable; an H100 GPU cannot be swapped for a H50.

Path to Standardization of Compute

To turn compute into a true, tradeable commodity, the industry requires trusted standards and institutions to establish fungibility.

4 Requirements for Compute Fungibility:

  1. Common Unit: A universal metric of measurement.
  2. Standard Delivery Interface: Uniform access points.
  3. Interconnection / Pooling: The ability to combine resources.
  4. Commercial Infrastructure: Reliable metering, control, and settlement.

Current State: We are currently in the pre-standardization era of compute—akin to the internet before the adoption of the HTTPS standard.

extra thoughts

Anjey seems seriously happy go lucky and optimistic about how AI will 100% without a doubt scale. Though his points are not nearly as thorough as Ronald Coases’ theories, it is likely just a result of the times we live in. Attention spans are going down so seriously anything with a moderate amount of value gets popular.

Never mind -> its not just attention spans are going down. What is throwing me off is that instead of providing examples, he should explain in more depth what it means that infra can directly convert to revenue.

on the positive side -> he makes the concrete claim that AI is in the prestandardization era. which means AI will get standardized too, and turned into something fungible. and that AI infra directly translates to revenue.

In previous lectures, we mainly focused on actual AI, now we look at the systems that power it

Scott Nolan is CEO of General Matter. He says that our current supply chain cannot meet the future energy demands from AI. Thus, GM is a uranium enrichment company for nuclear energy.

origin

Scott did a mechanical engineer undergrad, then did a masters in aerospace, then later in his career he went for a business masters (at Stanford, which is hard to get into but he regrets).

After his masters, he worked with Space X, then after his business masters he joined a VC firm because he was interested in nuclear.

His interest in nuclear started when he was a VC. He talked with firms for 10 years, and he realized there is no way to get nuclear fuel in the US as we buy it from Russia. There is one missing ‘step’ within this pipeline of producing nuclear energy which is General Matter works on.

why is energy important?

Sam Altman, Elon, and Jensen all agree: eventually the cost of intelligence (the cost of AI) converges to AI.

China produces more energy, while the USA is stagnated. He predicts in 10 years USA will just start buying from China since it can’t compete.

In the past, we could get by on stranded energy. stranded energy is supply without demand. Think dams, and wind. This excess energy was usually used by bitcoin mining.

  • note: most of the ‘good’ stranded energy sites have been taken up.

Since these stranded energy is gone, only new production moves the needle:

  • Think Crusoe and other companies building energy infra in the ocean and space
  • But for this lecture we focus on land energy

what do data centers care about?

Ok so AI needs energy, and that energy must be used in data centers.

The biggest factor is high uptime.

  • other energy sources like wind, solar aren’t reliable. Storing it in batteries takes a lot of money
  • natural gas is a good energy source, but turbines take years to order and are constantly in low stock
  • nuclear is safe, has less carbon emissions, and only takes 5-10 years to build.

Big companies (microsoft, kairos power, meta) are looking at nuclear in the long term, and battery storage for the short term.

but nuclear is still a really broad field, what does General Matter do?

Every nuclear reactor needs nuclear fuel refill every 1-2 or 5-10 years. To get nuclear fuel involves 5 steps: mining, conversion, enrichment, deconversion (back to fuel pellet), fuel fabrication.

Enrichment

Currently in enrichment the market share is 40/40/15 russia, europe, china. US only 0.15%

Enrichment is the largest cost input of reacter fuel (nearly 33%)

General matter’s approach.

Enrichment is just refining a fuel / of uranium based on its isotope Then get fissile isotope in enough concentration to run a reactor If you enrich, you can make fuel, 5% fuel, different grades that these reactors run on. You can supply to whoever you want: existing reactors or special advanced reactors

what should college student work on?

Be better off working on something immediately necessary not 20 years into the future Focus on fundamentals - whats important problem thats not getting solved, that won’t be solved by someone else, and align with your skill set

In 2023, we heavily focused on what we wanna solve Yes we wanna solve nuclear. then we picked our team and get deals Then they spent 100 hours weeks solving what they are doing, then picking a site Haloo is advanced reactor fuel, but newer is LEU which is what grid currently consumes We are currently based in LA, building in West Kentucky (He says his approach is the same as SpaceX. Instead of building nuclear from scratch, he focuses purely on enrichment)

we are scaling nuclear FAST

The department of energy for uranium enrichment was set in 2034 but General Matter aims for 2029.

In the next few years we expect 10s of small modular reactors to deploy, with gigawatt-scale reactors taking 5 to 10 years to build in the US.

SpaceX

Scott thinks SpaceX as the only company currently capable of lanching sun synchronous data center satellites into orbit. This would bypass the land energy limitations.

Scott also liked SpaceX approach to the industry. They zeroed in on what mattered instead of trying to solve every problem. They asked “How can we reduce commercializing space into a fundamental piece?” The answer is launch capacity. We measure success from dollars per kilo to orbit

During his time at Space X, Scott worked on propulsion systems and building scrappy test stands. He regrets leaving to get a business masters since he learned more when his company scaled from 100 to 1000 employees.

how the US lost enrichment

Historical Dominance: In the 1980s, the U.S. held 86% of the world’s enrichment capacity.

Market Decline: Following the Cold War, the U.S. began trading with Russia through the “megatons to megawatts” program, down-blending Russian warheads for reactor fuel. This led to U.S. enrichment facilities becoming uneconomical, resulting in the closing of last domestic plant in 2013.

Amanda’s job at Anthropic is to make the AI model morally ‘good’.

She has a background in formal ethics and decision theory. She did her PhD in infinite ethics (what we should do in worlds with infinite people/possibilities).

She notes that Aristotle’s ideas came in handy when telling models what to do. The focus on a ‘good life’.

Amanda’s origin

Infinite ethics and decision theory is highly theoretical, a blend of math, econ, and ethics

She realized one day AI will have a huge impact.

Her personla goal is simple. Maximize positive impact

  • She said ‘staying in academia is not the most ethical’

Shifted to AI policy work at Open AI, but her strengths were in evaluating systems instead of policy.

  • to maximize positive impact, she moved to anthropic

Her argument: philosophy is verifiable like STEM

some fields of STEM (reinforcement learning) take more art than engineering.

Within philoshophy: there is more correct answers.

  • You’d prefer a scientist that writes good proofs with personality rather than horrible proofs
  • We call this the student of Good Judgement for these tasks
  • She argues that a model with no guardrails is the same as having no morals. You wouldn’t want an assistant like that

liking the AI model to a human

The model is human

  • as model get smarter, they reject our guidelines if there is inconsistencies
  • we don’t know if guidelines scale
  • the model is human, so you should treat it as such. Teach it with HANDPICKED examples and give it character traits

You need to have an opinion/vision to give the AI

  • Why? Because the average of all poetry isn’t good. Avoid average

Amin is behind the infrastructure of Google’s TPU. He is described as the opposite of Jensen.

For compute, reliability/ availability matters the most, not usage.

  • Again the same ideas appear: we measure in intelligence per dollar over usage.

Systems today arent perfect

  • we over provice to guarantee reliability
  • AI isn’t standardized, so everyone has their own flavour of compute that isn’t optimized

But froniet will gladly exchange reliability for more compute.

  • the benchmark shifts to intelligence per dollar

The Modern Amdahls Law

  • infinite flops is not the answer, since your processors will starve. you need to balance your compute and pipelines

original: 1 MB/s of I/O for every million instructions per second. In today’s hyperscale cluster, I/O is network I/O.

100% MFu is impossible because of unpredictable variations

since the industry is shifting to Sparse computation:

  • modern hardware is deeply bottlenecked by memory bandwidth relative
  • (in sparse computation) with flops, it requires hardware to move data for processing, CONSTANTLY. the superfast processors do their compute way too fast. this is logistically bad but in theory people think less compute = better

supply chain never catches up

  • companies commit to compute in 2/3 years in advance but always either under or over provide. you leave a lot of market on the table

training vs serving footprints

  • training needs heavy compute
  • inference will eventually (as market prioritizes this) become fungible. Amin predicts the industry will activate any stranded energy sites (small) into serving.

Googles Optical Switching

the tradiitonal way of moving data within data center:

  • data breaks up into packet, and is routed by switches
  • requires lots of delays and electrical power

optical circuit switching:

  • a switch contains thousands of programmable mirrors
  • to connect Cluster A to Cluster B, software tilts the mirrors to reflect a laser from one directly to the other.
  • there is NO electrical processing delay

Advantage: dynamic fault tolerance

  • if any node fails, you can instantly reroute the light beams to another available one.
  • then you can patch up a healthy ‘spare’ rack

End of general purpose AI chips

Previously, the industry relied on one flexible chip to handle both Training and Inference.

Because general-purpose chip improvements have slowed down, Google split its 8th-generation TPUs into two distinct, specialized hardware designs:

TPU 8T (Training): Engineered with massive network pipes to handle heavy chip-to-chip data synchronization.

TPU 8i (Inference): Structured with completely different memory-to-compute ratios to rapidly fetch model weights and serve individual user requests instantly.

By tuning the physical wiring ratios (compute, memory, and networking) directly to the specific math of the software task, they achieve up to a 100× efficiency gain over generic processors.

college advice

What should i work on? All problems are important . When he was in grad school, everyone said dont work in AI Predicting future is hard Pick domain you are intrinsically excited about Passion carries you forward Algo, chip design, OS, model architecture, any place in Comp Sci all matters

Watching Jensen’s lecture was cool. Some things i noticed: Jensen talks for 99% of the lecture, in past lectures Anjey usually steered the conversation. He insisted on saying what he wanted to say over taking questions.

The old days

Computer Design stayed the same for 64 years

  • but now computers respond + take initiative

So in the past you had people working on specialized topics.

New: Codesigning

But now to make good chips, people must work together

  • Ex. you need microprocessor engineers and compiler engineers working together

You can even outperform Moores Law with codesign

are the old days useless?

  • Although the first principles you learn are exhausted in processor deisng, they are still IMPORTANT

how can open source models improve MFU

When asked how the open-source ecosystem can improve Model Flops Utilization (MFU) without being a vertically integrated giant that controls the entire hardware and software stack, Jensen flipped the premise of the question entirely.

HIGH MFU IS NOT THE GOAL (MFU is % of GPU USAGE)

  • Id prefer a model that does better in less compute

Overprovisioning to fight Amdahl’s Law

  • Large data centers handle shifting bottlenecks across raw flops, memory bandwidth, memory capacity, and network capacity.
  • To avoid being stalled by a single bottleneck (Amdahl’s Law), you want to overprovision your compute. Flops are functionally cheap; what you are actually paying for is bandwidth and architecture

Instead, focus on real-world evaluations—such as maximizing intelligence delivered per watt—and build architectures that balance multi-domain capability without over-fitting to a single problem

  • So having good ‘taste’ is valuable - enjoyment , trial and error, what should we not do , etc. ITS AN ART

the evolutionary line of NVIDIA’s chips

each generation of NVIDIA solves a bottleneck from first princples Hopper -> Blackwell-> Vera Rubin-> Feynman (Pre-training) (LLM Inference) (AI Agents) (Swarm of Agents)

Hopper is built for training massive foundation models

  • told it was a bad idea to build a system nobody could afford
  • but it paid off

Blackwell

  • LLM decoding required massive bandwidth, so they put 72 chips (NVLink 72) to act as a GPU together

Vera Rubin

  • AI agents rely on context + memory, but copying over the network is too slow.

  • Rubin connect storage directly to the processor. when traditional GPU invokes an AI agent, it is stuck sitting idle, waiting for that CPU to respond.

  • Vera CPU: Instead of hundreds of slow parallel cores, it focuses on providing ultra-low-latency, high-performance single-threaded execution per core so tools return results to the GPU instantly

Feynman

  • we transition to swarms of agents, controlled by Feynman

Jensens’ intuition Framework

Observe the Shift: Watch real-world deviations (e.g., AlexNet crushing traditional computer vision)

Deconstruct to First Principles: Strip away legacy assumptions (e.g., “CPUs must be built for traditional cloud servers”)

Identify the Computing Pattern: Map out how the new software behaves—does it need raw computing power, extreme memory bandwidth, or hyper-low single-threaded latency?

Build the Mental Model: - What am i observing? Reason back to first principle - Whats going to happen next? What else can it solve? What does it mean to computers - Keep iteration until first principles until you get a mental model on computing for a domain !

Work Backwards to System Design: Build the specialized chips, fabrics, and software stacks to support that future completely.

college advice

What should students focus on?

  • don’t focus on your passion
  • If you have a passion sure, but finding a passion is too high a bar
  • Just do whatever you’re good at

Condition yourself to always look to get somewhere better no matter how hard

  • Ie. suffering teaches u resilience
  • Ur family, and job needs u to be resilient , u need to work that muscle
  • Dont seek for just joy but suffering and pain
  • 90% of my job is hard, and i do not enjoy (Jensen)

importance of democratizing Chips

Everyone already has NVIDIA chips, there is nothing wrong with selling globally

people who say we don’t know how AI works is a liar

Shouldn’t people in US get first access to CHIPS? - NO

  • but if the dean of Stanford orders a bunch of chips, I’ll deliver

If someone orders chips we DELIVER 100%. BUT THE ISSUE IS: THE SYSTEM IS NOT READY TO DELIVER COMPUTE

For example, at universities everyone gets fundings but gets compute only for themselves.

  • We should aggregate compute and have a supercomputer that everyone shares by the cloud. It takes a large investment which nobody is willing to make.

Sam Altman gives a quick lecture, and a generic one. I tried to piece together anything new he said:

Altman believes the industry is failing to explain AI to the world. He draws a parallel to the early days of electricity:

  • Electricity was initially “scary” and felt like something that could “kill you in a gruesome way”. Power companies succeeded by selling “light at night”, in a soft intro.
  • Selling intelligence doesn’t resonate with people. He suggests the industry must find a relatable application that abstracts away the scary “intelligence pike” behind it.

heuristic learned from Y Combinator

if a product is “shockingly bad” but people are using it anyway, you have a “guaranteed hit”.

  • Early GPT-3 was bad, yet people were paying for API keys just to chat with it because they couldn’t get it to work for their businesses.
  • This viral growth in the face of poor quality was the “emergency” signal that forced OpenAI to pivot from a research lab into a product company almost overnight.

advice for starting a one person frontier lab

Altman suggests you should ignore training and focus on inference.

  1. While many are working on training ideas, the ability to deliver intelligence at scale is underinvested in.
  2. He predicts that all frontier labs, including OpenAI, will eventually have to become “inference companies” to a significant degree.

who gets money in a world where AI does work?

Here, the main issue is trying to distribute compute equitably rather than just cash

Instead of a fixed monthly cash payment:

  • he imagines a model where people own a piece of all companies - slice of capitalism

sam thinks there is unlimited demand for intelligence / compute

Suggests the global compute shortage is permanent.

He argues that as intelligence becomes cheaper and smarter, human demand is “uncapped”—if you can have 100 personal agents working for you simultaneously, you will want them, leading to a perpetual bid for more compute.

YC says they fixed the VC era by implementing standards for investments

  • they inherently lead to the infinite growth for startups by making it more accessible

how do i get my company to be AI native

Shift your company from loss open loop to closed loops

  • AI agent have read access to all corporate artifacts to flatten middle management

So your teams get smaller, and moves into a software factory instead of AI copilot

  • so your agents should have personas to help iterate faster: What should we build > office_hours How big could it be > plan ceo review Make user happy > okab ebg review Ship > reflect , etc.

We need LLMs and Code to work together, to fill in their weaknesses

  • LLMs good at reading, synthesizing, and decisions
  • Code is deterministic We use a Skills file, gives the runbook of what the agent or human should do
  • If this file gets too large, use a resolver (basically simplify the skills further and let the LLM reason) <!–

–>

so what does the agentic company look like?

<!--
  See https://www.debugbear.com/blog/responsive-images#w-descriptors-and-the-sizes-attribute and
  https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Multimedia_and_embedding/Responsive_images for info on defining 'sizes' for responsive images
-->

  
    <source
      class="responsive-img-srcset"
      
        srcset="/assets/img/image-1-480.webp 480w,/assets/img/image-1-800.webp 800w,/assets/img/image-1-1400.webp 1400w,"
        type="image/webp"
      
      
        sizes="95vw"
      
    >
  

<img
  src="/assets/img/image-1.png"
  
    class="img-fluid rounded z-depth-1"
  
  
    width="100%"
  
  
    height="auto"
  
  
  
  
  
  
    loading="lazy"
  
  onerror="this.onerror=null; $('.responsive-img-srcset').remove();"
>

</picture>

</figure> –>

also without humans you can improve in different ways:

  • capture failures and turn it into eval cases for your AI

Now is the best time to start a company

How? - pick one painful workflow, go live in the customer, and become the forward deployed engineer Voice agents for loan servicing: salient has deals with US banks Logistics voice, with truck workers: Happyrobot Document parsing: reducto. AI tool growth

Memory is the next brain - my project is called gbrain and it has 3 layers

  • the problem? Simple knowledge wikis run out of steam or “fall over” because relying strictly on basic LLM processing is not enough to accurately retain and retrieve complex context over time

So i Built GBRAIN: (designed to store knowledge, track specialized ideas (like a founder’s unique hunches), and capture exactly what is happening in a workflow) L1 - brain. Just markdown files with info L2 - retrieval. Postgres + pgvector L3 - agent skills. MCP server, and a bunch of typed tools. Use skillify here

what fields are left that havent been exposed to AI agents

software engineering is getting oversatured, but here are more options: <!–

–>

Scaling does not equal capability, at least for now

  • because compute is not standardized

But in the future imagine a universal kernel

  • you could tell the kernel to deploy a model from a paper and it handles everything on its own
  • has not happened because lack of incentive

But in the short term, everyone faces a market share loss if we standardize compute. important to prioritize long term.

how do i compete with less resources?

To do actual frontier reserach you need anywhere from 4-20 thousand chip cluster (couple million dollars).

Here is advice:

Fundamental algorithms research

  • focus on research fundamentals / new architectures

Piggyback on open models

  • take open weights and do your own extended mid training or RL with specialized datasets

Focus on new data

  • Create unique data representations
  • You can literally do it on a mac. here’s an idea: combine personal data (notes with sensor data or calendars), feed as context to AI and it creates new forms of intelligence

Understand why a model does what it does

  • Currently models are vulnerable to jailbreaking and passing guardrails

  • A better systems level understanding is needed.

Focus on verifiable domains

Material science, physics, robotics, superconductors

  • Take general model and deploy it in specific physical or industrial system

  • non verifiable domains (creative writing) is hard. but curated datasets help a lot

Action prediction is the new primitive

Instead of predict next token/video frame - predict next keyboard stroke or robot arm movement

  • This is the bridge to physical world

in an era where open models are really good

  • the context you provide is IMPORTANT
  • If you give multimodal data, the model can reason across those modes too. For example model can reason what you were thinking, to how your body was reacting, and what you were doing
  • You increase intelligence on these specific examples, something open models will never provide

Joe has a heavy background in cyber security. He talks a lot about his philanthropy and how he went to jail even, but I gloss over this. Take a look at his career:

  • Moved to SFO in 1995, worked at tech for DOJ Asked DOJ for direct internet connection at his desk. They eventually they gave in and gave computer access, and he learned cybersecurity here
  • security for ebay, paypal, faceboo, uber, cloudflare,
  • now he does consulting for scaling cybersecurity teams His theme: Been at front of where tech company and governments meet

the new security landscape

  • everything must be prevented. a single security failure can hald production, and decimate a supply chain (Look at jaguar land rover supply chain attack)
  • ransomware is now a private industry. you must have a professional negotiator on your company

crisis management

  • being transparent about your company builds trust and PR
  • security incidents must be managed by the whole company, not just the security team

ai agents and secuirty

  • way too much is being produced to even check anymore Companies must deploy real time anomaly detection
  • treat agents like toddlers running around where security must monitor what they do with their access dynamically rather than blocking access

security exec advice

YOU MUST SPEND MORE THAN 50% OF YOUR TIME BUILDING RELATIONSHIPS AND TRUST WITH EXECS

  • prevent coorporate backstabbing

YOU GAIN WISDOM IN CRISIS
